klReality.desc1: Kuala Lumpur is hot. It is humid. You sweat on the way to lunch, you reapply sunscreen, you sit in aircon, then you step out again. Your skin gets confused.
klReality.desc2: If you are reading this, you probably tried something already. Maybe a cleanser that felt too harsh. Maybe a "miracle" serum that worked for two weeks, then stopped.
klReality.step1: Identify what type of acne you have
klReality.step3: Know what your skin can tolerate
klReality.step4: Receive a plan you can actually follow

acneTypes.type1Desc: Tiny bumps, blackheads, and texture that never feels smooth. Often worsened by heavy makeup or humid days.
acneTypes.type2Desc: Strong skin reactions with redness, swelling, and tenderness. Needs targeted anti-inflammatory care.
acneTypes.type3Desc: Often shows around the jawline and chin in adults. Flares with stress, cycles, and sleep changes.
acneTypes.type4Desc: Sweat, friction, tight clothing, and gym habits all contribute. Requires a lifestyle-aware plan.
acneTypes.type5Desc: Dark marks that linger long after breakouts. Early treatment prevents long-term pigmentation.

phases.phase1Desc: Reduce inflammation, stop new breakouts, and simplify your routine to what actually works.
phases.phase2Desc: Once active acne slows, we target PIH and uneven tone while rebuilding your skin barrier.
phases.phase3Desc: Right habits, a minimal maintenance routine. No heavy products forever — just what your skin needs.

treatments.optCNote: Chosen by acne type & downtime tolerance.

firstVisit.desc1: A good acne plan always starts with three things: a clear diagnosis, a routine you can actually stick to, and treatments that match your goals.
firstVisit.desc2: You leave knowing what you likely have, what is making it worse, and what the first 4–8 weeks will look like — including what to do if dryness or purging happens.
firstVisit.step1Desc: We look at your acne type, location, inflammation level, and patterns that suggest hormones or irritation.
firstVisit.step2Title: A Routine You Can Stick To
firstVisit.step2Desc: Not a 9-step routine. Not 12 products. A realistic, followable plan.
firstVisit.step3Desc: Fewer breakouts fast? Fewer marks? Stopping cystic flare-ups? We choose based on your priorities and skin tolerance.

prosCons.pro1: Stops random trial-and-error with a structured plan
prosCons.pro2: Better control of breakouts and inflammation
prosCons.pro3: Reduced risk of marks and long-term scarring
prosCons.pro4: Guidance on what to stop doing — often the biggest win
prosCons.con1: Acne rarely disappears overnight — real change takes weeks
prosCons.con2: Some treatments cause early dryness or temporary purging
prosCons.con3: Severe acne may require longer monitoring and combination care

timeline.warning: If someone promises clear skin in 3 days, be careful.

faq.q1: How long does acne treatment take?
faq.a1: Most treatments need 6 weeks before you notice improvement. Prescription options take 4–8 weeks; some take 2–3 months for full effect. Anyone promising clear skin in 3 days is not being honest with you.
faq.q2: Why is my acne getting worse before it gets better?
faq.a2: Some actives — especially retinoids — cause a temporary flare as clogged pores clear. This is not failure. Pacing, moisturiser support, and the right frequency matter.
faq.q3: Can I treat acne with sensitive skin?
faq.a3: Yes. You need a gentler plan: start slow, avoid harsh scrubs, and focus on barrier support while introducing actives gradually.
faq.q4: When should I see a doctor for acne?
faq.a4: If your acne is painful, leaving marks, recurring, or affecting your confidence — don't wait. That is exactly the point at which a stronger plan makes a meaningful difference.
faq.q5: Is acne laser treatment worth it?
faq.a5: Laser helps certain acne types — especially inflammation-driven acne. It works best as part of a combination plan, not as a standalone.

cta.description: Start with an assessment, not a sales pitch. We identify what you have, what triggers it, and what the next 4–8 weeks look like.
